engineCodes.h2: Suzuki Engine Codes & Average Rebuilt Prices (UK Supply)
engineCodes.h3: Compare costs for every major Suzuki engine — K-series petrol, D-series diesel, and M-series units. All prices are typical UK market ranges for professionally rebuilt engines with warranty. Click "Get Quote" on any engine to receive tailored prices from our network of UK Suzuki specialists.
filters: M-Series to help users quickly navigate the engine families covered below.

groups.name: K-Series Petrol Engines
groups.failureNote: The most common Suzuki engine family in the UK. Known for timing chain stretch issues on early 1.2L variants. Common failure points: K-series petrol engines are known for timing chain stretch (especially K12B in 2011-2017 Swifts) and rattling on cold starts. Our rebuilt units include upgraded timing chain tensioners and genuine OEM-spec components.

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Alto (2009-2015), Suzuki Celerio (2014-2021), Suzuki Wagon R (2008-2012)
Suzuki Swift (2011-2017), Suzuki Ignis (2016-2020), Suzuki Splash (2008-2014), Suzuki Solio
Suzuki Ignis (2016-present), Suzuki Swift (2017-present)
Suzuki Swift (pre-2011), Suzuki Wagon R
Suzuki Swift Sport (ZC33S, 2017-present), Suzuki Vitara (2015-2024), Suzuki S-Cross (2013-2021)
Suzuki Jimny (JB74, 2018-present), Suzuki Ertiga (2018-present), Suzuki Ciaz (2015-2020)

groups.name: M-Series Petrol Engines
groups.failureNote: The enthusiast's choice — powers Jimny, Swift Sport, and SX4 through the 2000s. Robust but age-related failures common. Common failure points: M-series engines are generally robust but suffer from coil pack failures, cam position sensor issues, and oil leaks with age. Our rebuilt engines include new gaskets, seals, and tested ignition components.

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Jimny (JB23/JB43, 1998-2018), Suzuki Swift (ZC11S, 2005-2010), Suzuki Ignis (2000-2005)
Suzuki Swift (2005-2010), Suzuki Liana (2001-2007), Suzuki Ignis (2003-2010)
Suzuki Swift Sport (ZC31S/ZD31S, 2005-2010), Suzuki SX4 (2006-2013), Suzuki Vitara (pre-2015)
Suzuki SX4 (2006-2014), Suzuki Liana (2004-2007)

groups.name: G-Series Petrol Engines
groups.failureNote: The classic Jimny/Samurai workhorse. Simple, durable, but increasingly rare. High demand for off-road rebuilds. Common failure points: G-series engines suffer from carburettor issues (early models) and head gasket failure. Our rebuilds include new head gaskets and upgraded seals.

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Jimny (JB23, 1998-2005), Suzuki Swift (1995-2004), Suzuki Samurai (late models)
Suzuki Vitara (1990-2005), Suzuki Grand Vitara (pre-2005), Suzuki Samurai

groups.name: J-Series Petrol Engines
groups.failureNote: Found in larger Suzuki SUVs. Reliable but prone to timing chain rattle at higher mileage. Common failure points: J-series timing chains can stretch causing rattle and check engine lights. Our rebuilt units include new timing chains, tensioners, and guides.

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Grand Vitara (2005-2015), Suzuki Vitara (1998-2005)
Suzuki Grand Vitara (2006-2015), Suzuki Kizashi (2009-2014)

groups.name: D-Series Diesel Engines
groups.failureNote: Licensed from Fiat — these engines power many Suzuki diesels. Known for DPF and injector failures. Common failure points: D-series diesel engines suffer from DPF blockage, EGR valve failure, and injector problems — particularly on the D16AA in 2014-2019 Vitaras. Our rebuilt engines come with cleaned/replaced DPF and EGR components.

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Swift (2005-2017), Suzuki SX4 (2006-2013), Suzuki Splash (2008-2014), Suzuki Ignis (2016-2019)
Suzuki Vitara (2014-2019), Suzuki S-Cross (2013-2020)
Suzuki SX4 (2010-2014, Europe)

groups.name: H-Series V6 Petrol Engines
groups.failureNote: Found in premium Grand Vitara models. Complex but powerful — lower replacement volume. Common failure points: H-series V6 engines are known for timing chain tensioner wear and cooling system issues. Our rebuilds include new tensioners and water pumps.

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Grand Vitara (SQ416/420, 1998-2005)
Suzuki Grand Vitara (2005-2011, US/Europe)

groups.name: Small Capacity Engines — K6A / R06A / F6A
groups.failureNote: 660cc Kei car engines — primarily for Alto, Jimny (JB23), Carry, and Every models. Niche but high demand in off-road/import circles. Common failure points: These small turbo engines suffer from turbocharger wear and head gasket failures. Our rebuilt units include reconditioned turbos and upgraded gaskets

engines.compatibleModels: Suzuki Jimny (JB23, 1998-2018), Suzuki Alto (HA24/HA25), Suzuki Wagon R (MH21S)
Suzuki Jimny (JB64, 2018-present), Suzuki Alto Turbo RS, Suzuki Hustler

commonProblems.h2: Common Suzuki Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Suzuki owners are famously value-driven, and with good reason. While models like the Swift, Vitara, and SX4 are incredibly reliable daily drivers, specific engineering compromises — particularly the K12B timing chain and D16AA diesel emissions system — create predictable, high-mileage failure points. Below we break down the four most common failure clusters we see in the UK. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point, plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. This section is your decision tool: repair, replace, or compare marketplace prices.

problems.h4: K12B Timing Chain Stretch – The Most Common Petrol Engine Failure
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Swift (2011–2017, K12B 1.2L), Suzuki Ignis (2016–2020, K12C variant similar design)
problems.rootCause: The original timing chain tensioner relies on oil pressure and a weak ratchet mechanism. As the chain stretches, the tensioner cannot take up the slack, leading to chain rattle against the timing cover. If ignored, the chain jumps teeth or snaps, causing piston-to-valve contact and total engine seizure.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Preventative Timing Chain Set Replacement
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of timing chain, tensioner, guides, and camshaft sprockets. Oil and filter change included. No engine removal required. | 60,000+ miles if completed before chain jumps.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable for rattling but still drivable engines with no fault codes.

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt / Reconditioned K12B Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of a professionally rebuilt engine with upgraded timing chain components, new oil pump, gaskets, and bench testing. 12-month warranty included. | 80,000+ mile lifespan. Required if engine has jumped timing, has low compression, or suffered valve damage.

problems.recommendation: If you hear a metallic rattle on cold start that disappears as the engine warms, act immediately. A preventative chain set replacement at a specialist is the most cost-effective route for a Swift worth £2,000–£4,000. If the engine has already jumped timing or has a persistent misfire, do not waste money on chain-only repairs — the valves are almost certainly damaged. Go straight to a rebuilt engine.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt K12B engine prices for your Suzuki Swift or Ignis

problems.h4: D16AA Diesel Emissions Failure – DPF, EGR & Injector Collapse
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Vitara (2014–2019, D16AA 1.6 DDiS), Suzuki SX4 S-Cross (2014–2019, D16AA)
problems.rootCause: The D16AA diesel engine was designed primarily for European low-emissions compliance, but short journeys and low-speed driving cause repeated DPF blockages, EGR valve carbonisation, and eventually injector tip failure. Once the DPF fails to regenerate, soot backs up into the EGR and turbo, leading to complete emissions system collapse and engine contamination.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Forced DPF regeneration or off-car chemical clean, EGR valve removal and decoke. No engine-out work. | 20,000–30,000 miles with continued mixed driving.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if DPF light is on but no injector or turbo faults.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Removal and professional cleaning or replacement of DPF, EGR cooler, intake manifold, and injector refurbishment. | 40,000+ miles. Required if the car has gone into limp mode with multiple fault codes (P2002, P0401, P0200).

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Rebuilt D16AA Engine with Upgraded Injectors
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of a reconditioned engine with new or professionally rebuilt injectors, cleaned DPF/EGR system, and upgraded turbo oil feed. 12-month warranty included. | 80,000+ miles. Required if the engine has seized due to injector tip melt or DPF soot ingestion.

problems.recommendation: On a 2014–2017 Vitara worth £3,000–£5,000, a full engine replacement is rarely the first step. If the car still drives but has recurring DPF/EGR faults, invest in a specialist engine-out emissions system repair. However, if you have already replaced injectors or the DPF once and the car still fails, or if the engine has started knocking, cut your losses and compare rebuilt engine prices — the D16AA does not respond well to repeated patching.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt D16AA engines with upgraded injectors & cleaned emissions systems

problems.group: K14C Boosterjet Timing Chain & Turbo Failure
problems.h4: K14C Boosterjet Timing Chain & Turbo Failure – Swift Sport Weakness
problems.rootCause: The K14C is a high-specific-output turbo engine. Aggressive driving, poor oil maintenance, or ECU tuning increases heat cycling, leading to premature timing chain stretch and turbocharger bearing failure. The plastic chain guides are a known weak point under sustained high RPM.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of timing chain, tensioner, guides, and cam phasers. Engine remains in car. Turbo inspected but not replaced. | 50,000+ miles.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if the only symptom is cold-start rattle and no boost issues.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | New turbo cartridge (core), timing chain set, oil feed line replacement, and full oil system flush. | 60,000+ miles. Required if turbo has started whistling or smoking but engine compression is still good.

repairOptions.tier: | Full Replacement – Forged Rebuild / Reconditioned K14C
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of a reinforced engine with forged pistons, upgraded chain guides, and a new OEM turbo. Bench tested with 12-month warranty. | 80,000+ miles. The only sensible choice for tuned cars or engines that have suffered oil starvation and bottom-end knock.

problems.recommendation: If you own a standard Swift Sport and hear chain rattle, replace the chain set and tensioner immediately — do not wait. If the car has been remapped, do not waste money on partial repairs. Go directly to a forged rebuild or a specialist reconditioned engine with upgraded internals. The K14C does not tolerate half-measures on tuned cars.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt K14C Boosterjet engine prices for your Swift Sport

problems.h4: 1.6 DDiS Crankshaft & Bottom-End Failure – The Silent Killer
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Vitara (2014–2019, D16AA), Suzuki SX4 S-Cross (2014–2019, D16AA)
problems.rootCause: A subset of D16AA diesel engines suffer from oil pump drive failure or main bearing fatigue, particularly on cars with extended oil change intervals (18,000+ miles). Unlike timing chain rattle, this failure gives almost no audible warning before sudden crank seizure or bottom-end knock.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Engine removal, crankshaft regrind or replacement, new main and big-end bearings, oil pump replacement, and full gasket set. | 60,000+ miles.
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable if the crank has not scored the block and the car has very low mileage otherwise.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Supply & fit of a fully reconditioned engine with new crankshaft, upgraded bearings, and revised oil pump drive. 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. | 80,000+ miles. The recommended route for any D16AA with confirmed crank knock or oil pressure warning.

problems.recommendation: There is no minor fix for bottom-end failure. If your D16AA diesel starts knocking from the sump area or the oil pressure light flickers, stop driving immediately. On a Vitara worth £3,000–£5,000, a bottom-end rebuild is only worth it if the rest of the car is immaculate. For most owners, the logical, cost-effective answer is a fully rebuilt engine with warranty — compare specialist prices before committing to a dealer.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt D16AA engines with uprated bottom-end components

problems.h4: M13A & M15A Oil Burning – Jimny & Swift Petrol Consumption
problems.affectedModels: Suzuki Jimny (1998–2018, M13A 1.3), Suzuki Swift (2005–2011, M15A 1.5), Suzuki Ignis (2003–2010, M13A)
problems.rootCause: Piston ring wear and valve stem seal degradation, exacerbated by variable valve timing (VVT) oil control issues. The engine burns oil slowly, often going unnoticed until the oil level drops below the sump pickup, causing camshaft and bearing damage.

repairOptions.tier: | Minor – Valve Stem Seals & VVT Solenoid
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of valve stem seals and VVT solenoid. Cylinder head remains on engine. | 30,000–40,000 miles.
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if oil consumption is less than 1 litre per 1,000 miles.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Engine removal, cylinder hone, new piston rings, rod bearings, and full gasket set. | 60,000+ miles. Required if consumption exceeds 1 litre per 600 miles or blue smoke is visible.

| Supply & fit of a rebuilt engine with new rings, valve seals, and a revised VVT oil control system. 12-month warranty included. | 80,000+ miles. The most cost-effective route for Jimnys or Swifts worth £2,000–£4,000 with high oil consumption.

problems.recommendation: On an older Jimny or Swift, a full engine replacement is often cheaper than a dealer-requested piston ring job. Check your oil every 500 miles. If you are topping up more than 1 litre per 1,000 miles, skip the valve seal repair — it is a temporary fix. Go straight to a reconditioned engine from a specialist. Your car will run cleaner, stop smoking, and hold its value.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt M13A and M15A engine prices for your Jimny or Swift

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Suzuki
finalCta.paragraph: Whether you own a high-mileage Swift with a rattling K12B chain, a Vitara diesel with a blocked DPF, or a Jimny that burns more oil than fuel, the decision to repair or replace comes down to real-world costs — not dealer scare tactics. EngineMarket.co.uk makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Suzuki specialists for rebuilt, reconditioned, and used engines. Every engine supplied through our network includes upgraded components designed to fix the original failure point, plus nationwide delivery and a minimum 12‑month warranty. Stop guessing. Compare real prices and get back on the road without paying main dealer premiums. engineTypes.h2: Suzuki Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Suzuki engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle's age, condition and budget.

types.title: Suzuki Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,200 – £3,800 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Suzuki Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as uprated timing chain tensioners for K12B units and reinforced injector sealing for D16AA diesels – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Suzuki Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.title: Suzuki Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models like the Swift Sport or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Suzuki Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn't had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models like the Alto or Ignis where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Suzuki Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Suzuki specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £400 – £800 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Suzuki engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Suzuki model today.

engineSizes.h2: Suzuki Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Suzuki engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Suzuki 1.3L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.3L DDiS diesel engine was Suzuki’s entry-level oil-burner, fitted primarily to the Suzuki Ignis and Swift between 2003 and 2012. Known by the engine code D13A, this unit offered excellent fuel economy but suffers from blocked EGR valves and injector failure in higher-mileage cars. Replacement demand is moderate, driven by emissions system issues rather than bottom-end failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Suzuki 1.3L diesel engines

items.title: Suzuki 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6L DDiS (D16AA) is Suzuki’s most common and most troubled diesel. Fitted to the Vitara, SX4 and S-Cross between 2014 and 2019, this Fiat-sourced unit drives significant replacement demand due to DPF blockage, injector tip failure, and timing chain stretch. Owners searching for “Suzuki 1.6 diesel engine problems” are typically experiencing a complete emissions system collapse, which often makes a replacement engine the most cost-effective route
items.cta: Get quotes for Suzuki 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Suzuki 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0L DDiS engine (D20AA) was Suzuki’s largest diesel, fitted exclusively to the Grand Vitara between 2005 and 2015. While less common than the 1.6L unit, it suffers from similar emissions-related issues – particularly DPF clogging on short journeys and EGR cooler failure. Replacement engines are less frequently sought due to the Grand Vitara’s lower sales volumes, but supply is available through specialist breakers and reconditioners
items.cta: Get quotes for Suzuki 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Suzuki 0.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: Suzuki’s smallest modern petrol engine, the 0.6L unit (K6A) was designed for the Japanese kei car market but appeared in limited UK quantities in the Alto and Wagon R+. This three-cylinder engine is generally reliable but suffers from timing chain stretch in poorly maintained examples. Replacement demand is very low, but specialist suppliers do carry reconditioned units

items.title: Suzuki 0.7L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 0.7L turbocharged engine (R06A) was fitted to the Suzuki Alto from 2009 onwards as a fuel-efficient city car unit. This three-cylinder engine is generally robust, but the turbocharger and intercooler can fail on higher-mileage examples. Replacement engines are widely available due to the Alto’s popularity as a first car and runabout

items.title: Suzuki 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.0L three-cylinder engine (K10B) powered the Suzuki Alto and Celerio through the 2010s. Known for its excellent fuel economy, it suffers from timing chain stretch and, in some cases, piston ring wear leading to oil consumption. Replacement demand is moderate, driven by higher-mileage city cars where the cost of a chain replacement approaches the value of the vehicle

items.title: Suzuki 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L Dual VVT engine (K12B and K12C) is Suzuki’s most common petrol powerplant, fitted to the Swift, Ignis, Baleno, and S-Cross. Unfortunately, it is also the brand’s highest-volume failure point – timing chain stretch is endemic, typically occurring between 50,000 and 90,000 miles. A rattling noise on cold start is the classic symptom. Replacement engines with upgraded timing components are in very high demand

items.title: Suzuki 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.3L engine is legendary among Suzuki fans, primarily fitted to the Jimny, Swift, and Ignis across two decades. The M13A and G13BB engines are robust but suffer from high oil consumption as they age (worn piston rings and valve stem seals) and occasional timing chain issues. The M13A in the Jimny is particularly sought after – used engine prices remain strong due to off-road demand

items.title: Suzuki 1.4L Petrol Engines (Boosterjet)
items.description: The 1.4L Boosterjet turbo engine (K14C and K14B) powers the Swift Sport and Vitara, delivering strong performance from a small displacement. However, tuned examples suffer from timing chain stretch and turbocharger bearing failure. Enthusiasts searching for “Suzuki 1.4 Boosterjet engine problems” are typically looking for forged rebuilds or upgraded replacement units rather than standard reconditioned engines

items.title: Suzuki 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L engine (M15A) was Suzuki’s mid-range petrol for the Swift, Ignis, and Liana in the mid-2000s. It shares many characteristics with the M13A – it is fundamentally reliable but develops oil consumption and timing chain noise at higher mileages. Replacement demand is now low as most remaining examples are low-value cars, but reconditioned units are still available

items.title: Suzuki 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6L VVT engine (M16A and G16B) was fitted to the Grand Vitara, SX4, and earlier Vitara models. Known for being more robust than the 1.5L, it still suffers from timing chain stretch and, on the G16B, carburettor or fuel injection issues. The M16A in the SX4 is reasonably reliable, but replacement demand exists for high-mileage examples and off-road Grand Vitaras

items.title: Suzuki 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8L engine (M18A) is relatively rare in UK Suzukis, fitted only to the SX4 and Kizashi (in limited numbers). It shares the M-series family architecture and suffers from the same timing chain stretch issues as the 1.5L and 1.6L units. Replacement engines are less commonly sought due to low UK sales volumes, but specialist importers can supply reconditioned units
compatibleModels: Suzuki Kizashi (2009–2013 – very low UK volume)

items.title: Suzuki 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L petrol engine (J20A) was fitted to the Grand Vitara and Vitara before the diesels took over. This older design is robust but suffers from timing chain guide wear and, on some examples, oil pressure issues due to sludge buildup. Replacement demand is moderate, driven by high-mileage off-road Grand Vitaras where the engine has finally worn out

items.title: Suzuki 2.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.4L engine (J24B) was Suzuki’s largest four-cylinder petrol, fitted exclusively to the Grand Vitara between 2005 and 2015. While reasonably reliable, it suffers from timing chain stretch at higher mileages (100,000+ miles) and occasional problems with the variable intake manifold system. Replacement engines are available but less common due to the Grand Vitara’s relatively low sales of this specific variant

items.title: Suzuki 2.5L V6 Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5L V6 (H25A) was fitted to the Suzuki Grand Vitara XL-7 – a seven-seat version of the Grand Vitara sold in limited UK numbers between 2001 and 2006. This engine is rare in the UK; replacement demand is minimal. Most owners seeking “Suzuki 2.5 V6 engine” are looking for imported units from Japan rather than UK-stocked reconditioned engines
compatibleModels: Suzuki Grand Vitara XL-7 (2001–2006 – very low UK volume)

items.title: Suzuki 2.7L V6 Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.7L V6 (H27A) is an even rarer engine, fitted to the later Grand Vitara XL-7 and the Suzuki Vitara (Japan market only). UK-market examples are extremely limited. Replacement engines are specialist import items rather than mainstream stock. Most owners with failure will scrap the vehicle rather than replace the engine
compatibleModels: Suzuki Grand Vitara XL-7 (2005–2006 – extremely low UK volume)

engineSizes.closing: Not sure which engine size your Suzuki needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Suzuki specialists.

fuelTypes.intro: Choose your Suzuki engine by fuel type to compare realistic replacement prices, known brand-wide issues, and UK specialist supply – from the popular high-mileage petrols to the troublesome diesels and the latest mild hybrids.

items.title: Suzuki Diesel Engines
items.description: Suzuki diesel engines cover many of the brand's highest-mileage UK models. Replacement demand is usually driven by timing system wear, injector and turbo faults, plus EGR or DPF-related damage once repeat short-journey use starts affecting reliability.
knownFor: EGR valve carbonisation
injector seal leaks
DPF blockage
injector failure
importantNotes: Short-journey use increases DPF, EGR, and oil-dilution risk on many diesel engines.
A documented service history matters more on diesel replacements because injector, turbo, and timing faults often build gradually.

items.title: Suzuki Petrol Engines
items.description: Suzuki petrol engines span everything from core daily-driver models to higher-output performance variants. Most replacement enquiries start with timing chain noise, oil consumption, cooling-system failure, or internal wear that makes a warranted replacement more sensible than repeated repairs.
knownFor: Timing chain stretch
valve clearance issues
Turbocharger bearing wear
intercooler leaks
importantNotes: Cold-start rattles, overheating history, and oil use are the biggest warning signs before a petrol engine replacement becomes necessary.
Direct-injection petrol engines often need specialist attention for carbon build-up alongside the main mechanical repair.

fuelTypes.closing: Not sure which fuel type your Suzuki has? Enter your registration number above to instantly identify your model, engine code, fuel system and compatible replacement options – all backed by a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Suzuki specialists.

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Suzuki Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Suzuki engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Suzuki specialists.

entries.title: K10B 1.0L (996cc) Petrol
entries.description: K10B is commonly fitted to Suzuki Alto (2009-2015), Suzuki Celerio (2014-2021), Suzuki Wagon R (2008-2012). The most common Suzuki engine family in the UK.

entries.title: K12B 1.2L (1242cc) Petrol
entries.description: K12B is commonly fitted to Suzuki Swift (2011-2017), Suzuki Ignis (2016-2020), Suzuki Splash (2008-2014), Suzuki Solio. The most common Suzuki engine family in the UK.

entries.title: K12C 1.2L (1242cc) Petrol
entries.description: K12C is commonly fitted to Suzuki Ignis (2016-present), Suzuki Swift (2017-present). The most common Suzuki engine family in the UK.

entries.title: K14B 1.4L (1372cc) Petrol
entries.description: K14B is commonly fitted to Suzuki Swift (pre-2011), Suzuki Wagon R. The most common Suzuki engine family in the UK.

entries.title: M13A 1.3L (1328cc) Petrol
entries.description: M13A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Jimny (JB23/JB43, 1998-2018), Suzuki Swift (ZC11S, 2005-2010), Suzuki Ignis (2000-2005). The enthusiast's choice — powers Jimny, Swift Sport, and SX4 through the 2000s.

entries.title: M15A 1.5L (1490cc) Petrol
entries.description: M15A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Swift (2005-2010), Suzuki Liana (2001-2007), Suzuki Ignis (2003-2010). The enthusiast's choice — powers Jimny, Swift Sport, and SX4 through the 2000s.

entries.title: M16A 1.6L (1586cc) Petrol
entries.description: M16A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Swift Sport (ZC31S/ZD31S, 2005-2010), Suzuki SX4 (2006-2013), Suzuki Vitara (pre-2015). The enthusiast's choice — powers Jimny, Swift Sport, and SX4 through the 2000s.

entries.title: M18A 1.8L (1796cc) Petrol
entries.description: M18A is commonly fitted to Suzuki SX4 (2006-2014), Suzuki Liana (2004-2007). The enthusiast's choice — powers Jimny, Swift Sport, and SX4 through the 2000s.

entries.title: G13BB 1.3L (1298cc) Petrol
entries.description: G13BB is commonly fitted to Suzuki Jimny (JB23, 1998-2005), Suzuki Swift (1995-2004), Suzuki Samurai (late models). The classic Jimny/Samurai workhorse.

entries.title: G16B 1.6L (1590cc) Petrol
entries.description: G16B is commonly fitted to Suzuki Vitara (1990-2005), Suzuki Grand Vitara (pre-2005), Suzuki Samurai. The classic Jimny/Samurai workhorse.

entries.title: J20A 2.0L (1995cc) Petrol
entries.description: J20A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Grand Vitara (2005-2015), Suzuki Vitara (1998-2005). Found in larger Suzuki SUVs.

entries.title: J24B 2.4L (2393cc) Petrol
entries.description: J24B is commonly fitted to Suzuki Grand Vitara (2006-2015), Suzuki Kizashi (2009-2014). Found in larger Suzuki SUVs.

entries.title: D13A (1.3 DDiS) 1.3L (1248cc) Diesel
entries.description: D13A (1.3 DDiS) is commonly fitted to Suzuki Swift (2005-2017), Suzuki SX4 (2006-2013), Suzuki Splash (2008-2014), Suzuki Ignis (2016-2019). Licensed from Fiat — these engines power many Suzuki diesels.

entries.title: D16AA (1.6 DDiS) 1.6L (1598cc) Diesel
entries.description: D16AA (1.6 DDiS) is commonly fitted to Suzuki Vitara (2014-2019), Suzuki S-Cross (2013-2020). Licensed from Fiat — these engines power many Suzuki diesels.

entries.title: D20A 2.0L (1956cc) Diesel
entries.description: D20A is commonly fitted to Suzuki SX4 (2010-2014, Europe). Licensed from Fiat — these engines power many Suzuki diesels.

entries.title: H25A 2.5L (2495cc) Petrol
entries.description: H25A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Grand Vitara (SQ416/420, 1998-2005). Found in premium Grand Vitara models.

entries.title: H27A 2.7L (2736cc) Petrol
entries.description: H27A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Grand Vitara (2005-2011, US/Europe). Found in premium Grand Vitara models.

families.name: Small Capacity Engines — K6A / R06A / F6A

entries.title: K6A 0.66L (658cc) Petrol
entries.description: K6A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Jimny (JB23, 1998-2018), Suzuki Alto (HA24/HA25), Suzuki Wagon R (MH21S). 660cc Kei car engines — primarily for Alto, Jimny (JB23), Carry, and Every models.

entries.title: R06A 0.66L (658cc) Petrol
entries.description: R06A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Jimny (JB64, 2018-present), Suzuki Alto Turbo RS, Suzuki Hustler. 660cc Kei car engines — primarily for Alto, Jimny (JB23), Carry, and Every models.

entries.title: F6A 0.66L (657cc) Petrol
entries.description: F6A is commonly fitted to Suzuki Every (DE51V), Suzuki Carry (1985-1999), Jimny (JA11/JA12). 660cc Kei car engines — primarily for Alto, Jimny (JB23), Carry, and Every models.

engineCodeDirectory.closing: Can't find your engine code listed above? Use our full Suzuki engine code directory below — every code from the last 35 years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with warranty.

directory.h3: Full Suzuki Engine Code Directory (All Years)
directory.label: “View all Suzuki engine codes (40+ codes, 1985–2025)”
directory.intro: A comprehensive alphabetical listing of every Suzuki engine code from the last 35 years. Use the search above or enter your registration for an instant match to your specific engine. All codes link to a quote request.

engineYears.h2: Suzuki Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section maps every major Suzuki engine change across the full UK model range - from the late 1990s Jimny and Grand Vitara through the K-series and DDiS diesel era, the Boosterjet turbo generation, and into today's mild and full hybrid lineup - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year, match it to the correct engine code, and compare rebuilt or remanufactured replacement engine prices from verified UK Suzuki specialists.

years.preview: Jimny M13A, Grand Vitara H25A & Liana Era
years.description: Suzuki's late 1990s and early 2000s UK range was anchored by the Jimny 4x4, the Grand Vitara, and the Ignis city car - all powered by compact, mechanically straightforward DOHC petrol units that established Suzuki's reputation for uncomplicated, high-mileage reliability in the UK market.
keyChanges: Jimny (SN) launched in UK from 1998 with M13A 1.3-litre DOHC petrol - tough, compact unit designed for ladder-frame 4x4 duty
Grand Vitara (GT/GV) arrives with H20A 2.0 and H25A 2.5 V6 petrol units - the V6 offering genuine SUV refinement
Ignis (HT51) introduced with M13A 1.3 DOHC - a city car sharing the Jimny's engine architecture
Liana introduced with M16A 1.6 DOHC VVT petrol - Suzuki's first mainstream family saloon/estate for the UK
Wagon R+ continues with F10D 1.0 three-cylinder - straightforward city car architecture
mainEngines: M13A - 1.3 DOHC four-cylinder petrol (Jimny SN, Ignis HT51, Swift early)
M13A VVT (M13AA) - 1.3 DOHC VVT petrol (Jimny SN updated, Ignis Sport)
H20A - 2.0 DOHC four-cylinder petrol (Grand Vitara)
H25A - 2.5 V6 DOHC petrol (Grand Vitara V6)
M16A - 1.6 DOHC VVT four-cylinder petrol (Liana, Swift 1.6)
F10D - 1.0 three-cylinder petrol (Wagon R+)
knownFor: M13A is one of Suzuki's most robustly proven engines - hundreds of UK Jimnys are in active daily use well beyond 150,000 miles on original engines; the only systematic fault is VVT solenoid wear on post-2000 VVT-equipped variants
H25A V6 in the Grand Vitara is smooth and characterful but fuel consumption is considerable by UK standards - timing belt compliance and coolant maintenance are the primary service obligations
M16A in the Liana is a reliable family unit with minimal documented failure patterns - Suzuki's reputation for simple, durable DOHC engineering is well represented here
F10D three-cylinder in the Wagon R+ is basic but virtually unbreakable in urban use - throttle body deposits are the most common minor fault at higher mileage
years.cta: Search for 1998–2004 Suzuki Jimny, Grand Vitara, and Ignis replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Swift MK3, Grand Vitara 1.9 DDiS & Z-Series Diesel Introduction Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Suzuki introduced diesel power to the UK range for the first time via the 1.3 DDiS in the Swift and the 1.9 DDiS in the Grand Vitara - both sourced externally from Fiat and Renault respectively - while the new third-generation Swift brought the M15A and M16A petrol family to a fresh, sportier platform.
keyChanges: New Swift (RS416/ZA) launched in UK with M15A 1.5 and M16A 1.6 DOHC VVT petrol units - a more dynamic platform than the outgoing model
Swift 1.3 DDiS introduced - Fiat-sourced Multijet 1.3 four-cylinder diesel (Z13DTJ shared with Vauxhall Corsa)
Grand Vitara (JT) refreshed and receives Renault-sourced F9Q 1.9 dCi diesel - badged 1.9 DDiS in UK; first diesel Grand Vitara
Jimny retains M13A - unchanged architecture, Euro 3 and Euro 4 compliance maintained
SX4 (EY/GY) launched in UK with M16A 1.6 VVT petrol and 1.9 DDiS diesel option
mainEngines: M15A - 1.5 DOHC VVT petrol (Swift RS416)
M16A - 1.6 DOHC VVT petrol (Swift Sport ZC31S, SX4, Liana)
Z13DTJ (Fiat Multijet sourced) - 1.3 DDiS turbo diesel (Swift 1.3 DDiS)
F9Q (Renault dCi sourced) - 1.9 DDiS turbo diesel (Grand Vitara 1.9 DDiS, SX4 1.9 DDiS)
J20A - 2.0 DOHC petrol (Grand Vitara 2.0 J, SX4 2.0)
M13A VVT - 1.3 petrol (Jimny - continued)
knownFor: 1.9 DDiS (F9Q) in the Grand Vitara is the highest-risk engine in Suzuki's UK history - DPF blockage from short-journey driving, seized water pump (driven by timing belt), and injector cold-start failure are all documented faults; Honest John and UK owner communities advise budgeting for immediate timing belt and water pump replacement on any purchase

Swift 1.3 DDiS (Fiat Multijet Z13DTJ) is economical and provides strong torque for a small diesel - however DPF blockage and fuel injector seal wear are well-documented from 90,000 miles onward; not a unit Suzuki engineered itself

M16A in Swift Sport (ZC31S) is a free-revving naturally aspirated unit loved by enthusiasts - highly reliable, VTEC-rivalling high-rpm character, and minimal fault history makes it one of the most recommended first-sport-car engines in the UK
J20A in the Grand Vitara petrol is a simple, durable unit - petrol Grand Vitaras of this era are considerably more reliable ownership propositions than the diesel equivalents
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Suzuki Grand Vitara 1.9 DDiS engine replacement UK - Suzuki Swift 1.3 DDiS engine for sale - Suzuki Grand Vitara diesel engine supply fit - Grand Vitara DDiS reconditioned engine UK - Suzuki SX4 1.9 diesel engine replacement
years.cta: Find 2005–2008 Suzuki Swift, Grand Vitara, and SX4 replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: New Jimny 1.3 VVT, SX4 S-Cross & Euro 4/5 Petrol Updates Major Reliability Revision
years.description: Suzuki refined its core petrol engine family across the Jimny, Swift, and SX4 range for Euro 4 and early Euro 5 compliance, while phasing out the problematic 1.9 DDiS diesel from new sales and consolidating around proven in-house petrol technology.
keyChanges: Jimny updated with M13A DOHC VVT Euro 4 calibration - no architecture change but improved idle management and emissions compliance
Swift continues with M15A 1.5 and M16A 1.6 petrol - Euro 4 compliance tuning
Grand Vitara range simplified - 1.9 DDiS diesel continues in small numbers but focus shifts to 2.4 J24B petrol in UK
SX4 updated with 1.6 M16A and 1.9 DDiS; SX4 sedan variant limited UK sales
Kizashi (FR) introduced in UK as premium saloon - J24B 2.4 DOHC petrol unit, low UK volume
mainEngines: M13A VVT (updated) - 1.3 DOHC VVT Euro 4 (Jimny SN)
M15A - 1.5 DOHC VVT petrol (Swift ZA continued)
M16A - 1.6 DOHC VVT petrol (SX4, Swift Sport ZC31S)
J24B - 2.4 DOHC CVVT petrol (Grand Vitara 2.4, Kizashi)
F9Q (outgoing) - 1.9 DDiS diesel (Grand Vitara - run-out)
knownFor: J24B 2.4 in the Grand Vitara and Kizashi is a considerably more reliable ownership choice than the 1.9 DDiS - no documented systematic engine faults; CVVT variable valve timing operates cleanly and requires only oil interval discipline

M13A Jimny remains essentially unchanged and continues its reputation as one of the most dependable small 4x4 engines in production - ladder-frame design and simple drivetrain mean engine work is accessible to UK independent garages
Swift 1.5 M15A is notably smooth and economical by petrol supermini standards - timing chain (not belt), minimal ancillary failures, and strong longevity across UK ownership data

1.9 DDiS run-out examples continue to generate the majority of Suzuki diesel replacement enquiries during this period - DPF blockage and timing belt/water pump failure remain the defining fault patterns
years.cta: Compare 2008–2011 Suzuki Jimny, Swift, and Grand Vitara replacement engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: New Swift ZC72S/ZC32S, K12B DUALJET & Swift Sport Apex Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The fourth-generation Swift introduced Suzuki's new K12B 1.2-litre Dualjet engine - a dual-injector per cylinder design that delivered class-leading fuel economy for a naturally aspirated petrol supermini - while the Swift Sport ZC32S received the naturally aspirated M16A 1.6 in its finest state of tune.
keyChanges: K12B 1.2-litre Dualjet engine introduced in new Swift (ZC72S) - two injectors per cylinder, increased compression ratio to 11.5:1, water-cooled EGR, piston oil jets
Swift Sport (ZC32S) receives M16A 1.6 naturally aspirated 134 PS - enthusiast community widely regards this as one of the best modern NA sports car engines available
Dualjet K12B achieves up to 65.7 mpg official - among the most efficient petrol engines in the supermini class at launch
Jimny continues M13A VVT unchanged - Euro 5 compliant calibration from 2012
SX4 continues M16A 1.6 - no major engine change in this generation
mainEngines: K12B DUALJET - 1.2 DOHC petrol dual injector (Swift ZC72S - 94 PS)
M16A - 1.6 DOHC VVT petrol 134 PS (Swift Sport ZC32S)
M13A VVT - 1.3 Euro 5 (Jimny SN)
J24B - 2.4 DOHC (Grand Vitara continued)
M16A - 1.6 VVT petrol (SX4)
knownFor: K12B Dualjet is one of Suzuki's finest modern engine achievements - exceptionally fuel-efficient, smooth, and long-lived; no documented systematic failure patterns in UK ownership data, and What Car? reliability surveys consistently rank it at the top of the supermini segment

Swift Sport ZC32S M16A 1.6 NA is celebrated by UK driving enthusiasts - high-revving character, genuine handling depth, and near-bulletproof reliability make this the benchmark naturally aspirated Suzuki in the UK used market
K12B timing chain design (rather than belt) eliminates the scheduled belt replacement cost - combined with simple DOHC architecture and no turbocharger, ownership costs are among the lowest in class

SX4 M16A continues to demonstrate excellent durability but the model itself suffered declining UK sales - engine parts availability for future repairs remains strong given the K/M engine family's longevity in production
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Suzuki Swift 1.2 engine replacement UK - Suzuki Swift Sport ZC32S engine for sale - Suzuki K12B reconditioned engine - Suzuki Jimny 1.3 M13A engine supply fit - Suzuki Swift 1.2 Dualjet engine UK
years.cta: Get quotes for 2011–2014 Suzuki Swift K12B and Swift Sport M16A replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Vitara Boosterjet, S-Cross 1.4 & K14B Turbo Era Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Suzuki introduced turbocharged petrol technology to its mainstream UK lineup with the K14B 1.4 Boosterjet - a direct-injection turbocharged evolution of the K-series - deployed across the new Vitara and S-Cross, marking the brand's most significant petrol architecture change of the decade.
keyChanges: K14B Boosterjet 1.4-litre direct-injection turbocharged petrol introduced in Vitara and S-Cross - 140 PS, 220 Nm
New Vitara (LY) and S-Cross (JY) arrive as Suzuki's primary UK SUV models - both powered by K14B Boosterjet
ALLGRIP four-wheel drive system introduced alongside Boosterjet - electronically controlled AWD on Vitara and S-Cross
K10B 1.0 and K12B Dualjet continue in Swift and Celerio
Ignis (FF21S) introduced with K12C Dualjet engine and SHVS (Smart Hybrid Vehicle by Suzuki) mild hybrid - 12V lithium-ion belt-integrated starter-generator; Suzuki's first UK hybrid
mainEngines: K14B Boosterjet - 1.4 DOHC direct-injection turbo petrol (Vitara LY, S-Cross JY - 140 PS)
K12B DUALJET - 1.2 four-cylinder petrol (Swift ZC72S continued)
K12C DUALJET + SHVS - 1.2 mild hybrid petrol (Ignis FF21S)
K10B - 1.0 three-cylinder petrol (Celerio)
M13A VVT - 1.3 petrol (Jimny SN - continued to 2018)
knownFor: K14B Boosterjet is a characterful, punchy turbo unit - timing chain wear and fuel injector seal leaks are documented faults from 60,000 miles onward, requiring prompt attention to avoid oil contamination
K14B head gasket failure has been reported on Vitara S examples, particularly when cooling system maintenance has been deferred - thermostat and coolant pump condition are pre-purchase priorities

Ignis K12C with SHVS mild hybrid is an elegant introduction to hybrid technology - the 12V SHVS system is mechanically simple compared to full hybrids, with very few documented failures in the UK specialist community

Jimny M13A completes 20 years in near-unchanged UK production - its longevity is a testament to the engine's fundamental robustness, and the global Jimny community supports excellent parts availability for any repair
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Suzuki Vitara 1.4 Boosterjet engine replacement UK - Suzuki S-Cross 1.4 engine for sale - Suzuki K14B reconditioned engine UK - Suzuki Vitara Boosterjet engine supply fit - Suzuki Ignis engine replacement UK
years.cta: Find 2015–2018 Suzuki Vitara and S-Cross Boosterjet replacement engines from UK engine specialists

years.preview: New Jimny J15B, Swift Sport ZC33S & SHVS Expansion Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Suzuki launched the all-new fourth-generation Jimny in 2018 with a new 1.5-litre K15B petrol engine replacing the long-running M13A, introduced the turbocharged K14C Boosterjet in the Swift Sport ZC33S, and expanded SHVS mild hybrid across the Swift and Vitara mainstream range.
keyChanges: New Jimny (JB74) launched in UK with K15B 1.5-litre DOHC VVT petrol - replaces M13A after 20 years; 102 PS, improved emissions and efficiency
Swift Sport ZC33S arrives with K14C Boosterjet 1.4 direct-injection turbo 140 PS - different from K14B, updated architecture
Swift ZC53S updated with K12C Dualjet + SHVS mild hybrid as standard - 12V system across all new Swift models
Vitara updated from K14B to K14D 48V Boosterjet MHEV from 2020 - 48V ISG replaces 12V SHVS on S-Cross and Vitara
SHVS 12V system expanded to Ignis, Swace (Toyota Corolla hybrid platform), and Across (RAV4 PHEV platform)
mainEngines: K15B - 1.5 DOHC VVT petrol 102 PS (Jimny JB74)
K14C Boosterjet - 1.4 turbo direct-injection petrol 140 PS (Swift Sport ZC33S)
K12C DUALJET + SHVS 12V - 1.2 mild hybrid petrol (Swift ZC53S)
K14D Boosterjet 48V MHEV - 1.4 turbo + ISG (Vitara, S-Cross - from 2020)
2ZR-FXE (Toyota sourced) - 1.8 full hybrid (Swace)
A25A-FXS (Toyota sourced) - 2.5 PHEV hybrid (Across)
knownFor: K15B in the new Jimny is a notable upgrade over the M13A in refinement and economy - early UK examples show strong reliability with no documented systemic faults; the Jimny's cult status ensures the K15B will generate a large pool of specialist rebuilders over time

K14C Boosterjet in Swift Sport ZC33S is regarded by UK driver communities as more sophisticated than the ZC32S naturally aspirated M16A, though enthusiasts note the turbocharged character is less pure; vacuum pump recall affected early production

K14D 48V Boosterjet MHEV is the most refined Boosterjet variant - the 48V ISG meaningfully reduces urban fuel consumption and the ISG system itself is technically more robust than the earlier 12V SHVS

Across PHEV (Toyota RAV4-derived) is mechanically identical to the RAV4 PHEV - UK owners benefit from Toyota's extensive hybrid drivetrain reliability data; this is Suzuki's highest-technology UK product
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Suzuki Jimny 1.5 K15B engine replacement UK - Suzuki Swift Sport 1.4 turbo engine for sale - Suzuki K14C Boosterjet reconditioned engine - Suzuki Vitara 48V MHEV engine supply UK - Suzuki Jimny JB74 engine replacement UK
years.cta: Compare 2018–2021 Suzuki Jimny, Swift Sport, and Vitara MHEV replacement engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: e-Vitara Electric, K14D MHEV Maturity & Full Hybrid Vitara Technology Introduction
years.description: Suzuki completed its electrification roadmap with the launch of the all-electric e-Vitara in 2025 - built on a platform co-developed with Toyota - while the K14D Boosterjet MHEV became fully mature across the Vitara and S-Cross, and the Vitara received a full 1.5-litre strong hybrid option.
keyChanges: e-Vitara (BH8) launched in UK (2025) - 49 kWh or 61 kWh battery, single and dual motor options up to 180 PS; co-developed with Toyota on HEARTECT-e platform
Vitara receives 1.5 Dualjet full strong hybrid (K15C + MGU + 140V lithium-ion) - alongside continued 1.4 Boosterjet 48V MHEV option
S-Cross continues with K14D Boosterjet 48V MHEV - sole engine for UK market
Swift AZ (2024 generation) updates to K12D 1.2 Dualjet MHEV 83 PS - revised K-series with 12V mild hybrid as standard
Across discontinued in UK; Swace Toyota-based 1.8 hybrid remains in limited trim
mainEngines: e-Vitara electric motor - 142 PS / 172 PS / 180 PS AWD (e-Vitara BH8 - 49 kWh and 61 kWh)
K15C DUALJET + MGU - 1.5 strong hybrid (Vitara Full Hybrid)
K14D Boosterjet 48V MHEV - 1.4 turbo 129 PS (Vitara MHEV, S-Cross MHEV)
K12D DUALJET MHEV - 1.2 mild hybrid 83 PS (Swift AZ - 2024 generation)
K15B - 1.5 petrol (Jimny JB74 - continued)
knownFor: e-Vitara represents Suzuki's most significant UK product launch in a generation - Toyota-co-developed platform carries the dual benefit of EV refinement and the reliability legacy of Toyota's battery technology; long-term UK data is still emerging

K15C strong hybrid in the Vitara is a technically mature system - Toyota's full hybrid architecture embedded in a Suzuki-branded product means owners benefit from one of the most proven strong hybrid systems available in this price bracket

K12D MHEV in the 2024 Swift is an evolutionary refinement rather than a radical change - What Car? 2024 reliability survey placed Suzuki 3rd out of 31 manufacturers, reflecting the cumulative strength of the K-series engine philosophy

K14D Boosterjet timing chain wear on higher-mileage examples (inherited from K14B/K14C architecture) remains a watchpoint - specialist advice is to shorten oil change intervals to 6,000 miles and use 0W-20 specification oil to protect the chain tensioner
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Suzuki Vitara 1.4 MHEV engine replacement UK - Suzuki Swift 1.2 MHEV engine for sale UK - Suzuki S-Cross engine supply fit - Suzuki Jimny K15B engine replacement - Suzuki K14D Boosterjet engine UK specialist
years.cta: Get quotes for 2022–2025 Suzuki e-Vitara, Vitara Full Hybrid, and Swift MHEV replacement engines from verified UK specialists

faq.h2: Suzuki Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: ** Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Suzuki owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Suzuki engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Suzuki engine typically costs between £1,500 and £3,500 for a reconditioned unit, depending on the model. A common K12B 1.2L petrol from a Suzuki Swift costs around £1,600–£2,500 supply & fit from a specialist, while a K14C Boosterjet for a Swift Sport ranges from £2,800–£4,200 with upgraded internals. Used engines start lower (£600–£1,500) but carry higher risk. Main dealer replacement can exceed £5,000–£7,000 on newer models. Always compare at least three supplier quotes – prices vary significantly for the same engine code.
items.cta: Compare Suzuki engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Suzuki engine replacement?
items.answer: Four factors drive the final price: engine size and rarity (e.g., a K14C Boosterjet costs more than a high-volume K12B), condition (rebuilt with warranty vs used as-is), vehicle complexity (a Grand Vitara engine swap is simpler than a Swift Sport with hybrid integration), and supply-only vs fully fitted. Location also matters – labour in London or the South East can be 20–30% higher than the Midlands or North. Our comparison tool breaks down exactly what’s included so you see the real price before you commit.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Suzuki engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Suzuki?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and body are solid. A reconditioned Suzuki engine with upgraded components (e.g., K12B chain fix, D16AA injector revisions) often outlasts the original. The alternative is scrapping a £3,000–£6,000 car and buying another used vehicle with unknown history. On a well-maintained Swift, Vitara, or Jimny, replacement is almost always the cheaper, smarter choice. If your car holds value (e.g., Jimny, Swift Sport), replacement is a no-brainer.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Suzuki engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: What's included in a Suzuki engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply-and-fit service includes sourcing the correct engine code (e.g., K12B, D16AA, M13A), removing your old unit, installing the replacement, all new fluids, filters, gaskets, and a workmanship warranty – typically 12 months. The supplier handles the old engine for disposal, road-tests the vehicle, and ensures it meets UK emissions standards for your model year. Fitting alone typically adds £500–£900 depending on the engine bay complexity. Always confirm whether VAT, delivery, and ancillaries like the clutch or timing belt are included.
items.cta: Compare Suzuki engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a Suzuki engine replacement take?
items.answer: For a common Suzuki like a Swift 1.2L (K12B) or Vitara 1.6L (D16AA), a specialist typically completes the job in 3–5 working days. More complex models – a Grand Vitara with 4×4 systems or a Swift Sport with a turbo and hybrid SHVS – may take 5–7 days. The biggest variable is engine availability: our network stocks commonly replaced codes like K12B, K14C, and D16AA for next-day dispatch, but rarer units (H25A V6, D20AA diesel) may add 2–3 days for sourcing. Ask your chosen supplier for a firm lead time before booking.
items.cta: Compare Suzuki engine replacement prices now

items.question: Do reconditioned Suzuki engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every reconditioned Suzuki engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12-month, unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many specialists offer extended 24-month coverage on rebuilt and remanufactured units for an additional premium. The warranty typically covers major internal components: crankshaft, pistons, conrods, timing chain, oil pump, and cylinder head. Used engines may come with 1–3 months only. Always confirm exact terms – what’s covered, what’s excluded, and claim procedures – directly with your supplier before purchase.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Suzuki engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Suzuki engine?
items.answer: A used engine is cheaper upfront (£600–£1,500) and makes sense for a temporary fix or if you plan to sell the car within 12 months. However, it carries the same original weaknesses. A reconditioned engine (£1,500–£3,500 supply & fit) includes new piston rings, bearings, timing chain, gaskets, and often upgraded components to fix the original failure point – plus a 12-24 month warranty. If you own a K12B Swift, D16AA Vitara, or tuned K14C Swift Sport, a reconditioned unit with upgraded internals is strongly recommended.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Suzuki engine makes financial sense

items.question: Where can I find Suzuki engine specialists near me?
items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK-wide network of independent Suzuki engine specialists who stock your specific engine code. While there are no major regional hubs exclusively for Suzuki, experienced independents are concentrated in the Midlands, North West, and South East – but our network covers all UK regions. Simply enter your registration or engine code, and we match you with nearby suppliers who offer fitting. Nationwide delivery is also available if you use your own mechanic.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Suzuki engine specialists

items.question: What are the most common Suzuki engine failures?
items.answer: The most common failures that drive Suzuki engine replacement are: K12B 1.2L petrol timing chain stretch (2011–2017 Swift, chain rattle then jump), D16AA 1.6L diesel DPF/injector failure (2014–2019 Vitara, S-Cross, soot ingestion then seizure), K14C Boosterjet timing chain stretch on tuned Swift Sports, M13A/M15A oil burning from piston ring wear (Jimny, Swift), and D20AA diesel DPF/EGR issues on the Grand Vitara. These failures rarely respond to minor repairs once symptoms appear – replacement is often the only lasting solution.
items.cta: Compare Suzuki engine replacement prices now

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Suzuki engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes – prices for the same engine code and workshop type vary by £1,000+. For tight budgets, a used engine with short warranty can work; for long-term ownership, a reconditioned unit with upgraded components is better value. Ask for supply-only if you have a trusted garage and compare supply-and-fit packages as a bundle. Always check whether fitting, delivery, oil, filter, and VAT are included in the quoted price. Use EngineMarket’s free comparison tool to see real-time specialist pricing for your exact Suzuki model.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Suzuki engine makes financial sense
